HANDOFF PROMPT — MetaClaude Embedding Index Build                 
  Date: 2026-03-10
  Previous session: ~3.5 hours, brainstorm + de-risk + build        
                                    
  === GOAL ===                                 
                       
  Build the retrieval layer (Phase 2) for MetaClaude — a
  metacognitive
  observer agent that watches Claude Code sessions and makes
  selective
  context injections. The embedding index lets the observer retrieve
  relevant chunks from the learner's state, design docs, notepad,
  and
  reference materials when making observations.

  The broader arc: Phase 2 (embedding index) → Phase 2.5 (fine-tune
  a local model on retrieval-aware training data) → Phase 4
  (empirical
  comparison framework).

  === WHAT'S DONE ===

  Phase 2 pre-flight (all 4 de-risk steps complete):
  - sqlite-vec + bun:sqlite: BLOCKED (bun doesn't support
  loadExtension).
    better-sqlite3: BLOCKED (not supported in bun). Resolved: Vectra
    (pure TypeScript, brute-force cosine sim) behind an interface
  boundary.
  - LM Studio embedding endpoint: WORKING. nomic-embed-text returns
    768-dim vectors. Model ID: text-embedding-nomic-embed-text-v1.5.
    All inference + embedding models coexist on localhost:1234.
  - Chunking strategy: documented in PRD. Auto-detection per file
  type.
    current-state.md chunks per-concept, arcs.md per-section,
  notepad
    per-file or per-heading, etc.
  - Retrieval test cases: 7 tests written in
    tools/embedding/retrieval-tests.md with Fast + Deep query
  variants.

  Phase 2 build (core complete, integration remaining):
  - tools/embedding/store.ts — VectorStore interface + VectraStore
  impl.
    The interface (Chunk, StoredChunk, VectorStore) is the swap
  boundary.
    To switch to usearch later, implement VectorStore, change the
  factory.
  - tools/embedding/embed.ts — embed() and embedBatch() via LM
  Studio
    /v1/embeddings. Also checkEmbeddingHealth().
  - tools/embedding/chunker.ts — chunkSource() with auto-strategy
    detection. Strategies: yaml-concepts (current-state.md),
  yaml-arcs,
    markdown-sections (most files), whole-file (short files).
  - tools/embedding/index.ts — CLI: crawl sources, chunk, embed,
  store.
    Flags: --reindex, --stats, --dry-run. Sources configured for
  roger/,
    weft-dev/, weft/.
  - tools/embedding/query.ts — CLI + library. queryIndex() returns
    results with score, source, section, text, formatted (breadcrumb
    trail). Exports for observer.sh integration.
  - Index built: 685 chunks, stored at
  ~/.claude/metaclaude/embedding-index/
  - Retrieval validated: all 7 handcrafted tests hit expected
  targets
    in top 3 on Deep-mode queries.

  === WHAT'S NOT DONE (priority order) ===

  1. BROADER RETRIEVAL QUALITY TEST (was about to start when session
   ended)
     Task: find 10 real conversation windows from Claude Code
  session
     transcripts (~March 3-7). Transcripts are at
     ~/.claude/projects/*/conversations/*.jsonl. For each window,
  extract
     ~10 substantive turns (user text + assistant text, tool calls
     collapsed to summaries). Generate a Deep-mode query that a
  model
     observing the conversation might produce. Run against the
  index:
       bun tools/embedding/query.ts --top 3 --threshold 0.6 "query"
     Evaluate: are results meaningfully useful, or noise? Would the
     meta-agent be able to turn them into actionable injections?
     IMPORTANT: don't inject knowledge the meta-agent wouldn't have
  at
     runtime. The observer sees only recent_turns + accumulator.

  2. UPDATE prompt.md (weft/tools/metaclaude/prompt.md)
     Add instruction: when observation draws on retrieved content,
  include
     source path as (ref: path) so Builder Claude can look up full
  context.
     See "Source attribution (breadcrumb trail)" section in PRD.

  3. WIRE RETRIEVAL INTO OBSERVER PIPELINE
     observer.sh currently sends: recent turns + accumulator + turn
  count.
     Add retrieval step between fingerprint check and inference:
     - Embed the recent transcript window
     - Query the index (top 3-5, threshold ~0.7)
     - Format results with source paths:
       "[Retrieved from learning/current-state.md — concept:
  react-context]
       score: 2, gap: conceptual, ..."
     - Include in the payload sent to the inference model
     query.ts already exports queryIndex() and returns formatted
  results.
     The observer is a bash script (observer.sh) — it will need to
  call
     bun tools/embedding/query.ts and capture stdout, or the
  retrieval
     step could be a separate script the observer calls.

  4. PHASE 2.5: FINE-TUNE META-AGENT (deferred, not this session)
     Full spec in PRD. Depends on: embedding index running in
  production
     for 3-10 sessions to accumulate training data. Training data
  pipeline:
     read session logs, extract context_window, call Opus to produce
   ideal
     <inject>/<context> responses, format as chat JSONL for MLX-LM
  LoRA.
     Target: 4B or 8B Qwen3, trained on actual production pipeline
  input
     (recent turns + accumulator + retrieved chunks). See PRD Phase
  2.5.

  === KEY DECISIONS (all documented in PRD) ===

  - Vector store: Vectra (pure TS) behind interface boundary.
  usearch
    (native HNSW) deferred pending security review of prebuilt
  binaries.
    Swap at >10K chunks if needed.
  - No sqlite-vec, no better-sqlite3 (both incompatible with bun).
  - Score threshold only for relevance filtering (no tag-based
    deprioritization). Post-retrieval re-ranking deferred as future
  option.
  - meta/ session logs EXCLUDED from index (prevents feedback loop —
    meta-agent's own past observations would compound errors).
  - Source attribution (breadcrumb trail): chunks carry source path
  +
    section through retrieval → meta-agent → injection → Builder
  Claude.
    Builder Claude can Read the source file for full context.
  - Fine-tuning deferred to Phase 2.5 (after embedding index is
    battle-tested). 1.7B model dropped — too small for the actual
    meta-agent payload. Target: 4B or 8B via MLX LoRA.
  - Chunking: auto-detection per file type. ~685 chunks across all
    sources. Re-indexing daemon deferred to Phase 5 (chokidar v5
    preferred, manual reindex script sufficient for now).
  - Observation from testing: Deep-mode queries (model-generated,
    targeted) produce much better retrieval than Fast-mode queries
    (raw transcript). This supports the PRD's hypothesis that
    model-directed retrieval surfaces pedagogically relevant content
    that cosine similarity over raw transcript misses.

  === KEY FILES ===

  PRD: design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md (heavily updated
  this session)
  Embedding tools:
  tools/embedding/{store,embed,chunker,index,query}.ts
  Retrieval tests: tools/embedding/retrieval-tests.md
  Observer:
  /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft/tools/metaclaude/observer.sh
  Meta-agent prompt:
  /Users/rhhart/Documents/GitHub/weft/tools/metaclaude/prompt.md
  Index data: ~/.claude/metaclaude/embedding-index/
  Source directories indexed: roger/ (learning, notepad), weft-dev/
  (design, research), weft/ (references)

  === DEPENDENCIES ===

  - bun (runtime)
  - vectra (npm, installed in weft-dev)
  - LM Studio running on localhost:1234 with nomic-embed-text loaded
  - better-sqlite3 and sqlite-vec are installed but UNUSED (blocked
  by
    bun incompatibility). Can be removed from package.json.

  === BLOCKERS ===

  None active. The broader retrieval test (step 1 above) is the next
  task. If retrieval quality is poor on real transcripts, the
  threshold
  and top-K parameters need tuning before wiring into the observer.

turns.text: Clean results. Let me write up the assessment.

Here's the summary:

**Retrieval quality test: 8/10 queries return actionable results.** 3 excellent (direct hits, 0.76-0.82), 5 good (relevant context, 0.71-0.74), 2 mixed (adjacent but not tightly targeted).

**Key findings:**
- Deep-mode queries confirm the PRD hypothesis — model-generated analytical queries produce much better retrieval than raw transcript
- The two misses are **content gaps**, not retrieval failures — the knowledge exists only in conversations, not in indexed docs
- Threshold 0.65-0.70 is safe for the observer pipeline

**Bug fixed:** Vectra's `queryItems(vector, query, topK)` signature was being called as `queryItems(vector, topK)` — topK was being treated as a BM25 search string, so every query returned unlimited results. Fixed in `store.ts:83`.

**Verdict:** Retrieval quality is sufficient to wire into the observer pipeline. Ready to proceed with tasks #2 (update prompt.md) and #3 (wire retrieval into observer.sh).

Want me to continue to task #2?

Return to the PRD and see if you can tell did the previous working agent that structured the query scripts and query items have the same confusion about the top K? If said confusion is written into the architecture, we need to fix it.
Let me check all the places where top-K, retrieval parameters, or query invocations are specified.

The bug is **already fixed** in `store.ts:83` (the fix I applied earlier this session). The confusion doesn't appear to be baked into the PRD or any other architecture docs — those just say "top-K" and "threshold" as abstract parameters, not code-level API calls.

Let me check: did the previous session's handcrafted retrieval tests (the 7 tests that "all hit expected targets in top 3") actually run with the bug active? Because if so, those results were against unlimited results, not top 3.

Right — the embedding tools aren't committed yet. So the previous session wrote `store.ts` with the bug, validated the 7 handcrafted tests against it (getting unlimited results, not top 3), and then the PRD recorded "all hit expected targets in top 3."

Here's the damage assessment:

**The bug was present during the original 7 handcrafted tests.** The claim "all hit expected targets in top 3" was technically true — the expected targets *were* in the top 3 — but the tests didn't verify that *only* 3 results were returned. The test methodology was "at least 1 expected target in top 3," which works regardless of how many total results come back. So the quality finding holds, but the top-K limiting was never actually tested.

**Where it matters going forward:**
1. **observer.sh integration (task #3)** — the observer will call `queryIndex()` to get top 3-5 chunks for the meta-agent payload. Without the fix, it would have dumped hundreds of chunks into the model's context, blowing the payload budget and drowning the signal.
2. **PRD line 366** says "threshold 0.6 still returns many results; ~0.7 with top-K 3-5 recommended for production" — the "many results" observation was the symptom of this bug. At threshold 0.6 *with working top-K*, you get exactly K results (all above threshold). The threshold recommendation should be revisited now that top-K actually works.

**The PRD itself is fine** — it describes the architecture in terms of "top-K" and "threshold" correctly. The confusion was only in the Vectra API call in `store.ts`. No other code or docs reference the `queryItems` signature directly.

Want me to update the PRD's Phase 2 notes to document the fix and correct the threshold observation?

yes. and this means we need to improve the test methodology to check that results are validated, not just counted. And that the returns on these searches are not overwhelming to the working agent or meta-agent that deals with the actual content.

Now let me also update the retrieval-quality-test.md to reflect the truncation fix and add a payload validation section.

Here's what I did:

**PRD updates:**
- Status line: documents the Vectra topK bug, that handcrafted tests checked presence not count, and that it's fixed
- Architecture diagram: corrected stale "SQLite-vec" to "Vectra"
- Phase 2 build: revised the handcrafted test note to acknowledge the bug was present during testing, corrected the threshold observation
- Phase 2 build: marked broader test complete with results summary
- Decisions table: added two new rows — retrieval payload budget (800 chars/chunk, ~2500 total) and Vectra API contract (documenting the correct parameter order)

**query.ts:**
- Added `MAX_CHUNK_CHARS = 800` with truncation at paragraph boundaries
- Both `text` and `formatted` fields are now truncated
- Truncation marker tells the downstream agent the source path has more

**retrieval-tests.md:**
- Added validation rules section: tests must check (1) result count matches top-K, (2) total payload stays within budget, (3) content relevance. Explains *why* — the consuming agent's context is finite, and an unbounded retrieval silently degrades observation quality.

**retrieval-quality-test.md:**
- Added payload size comparison table (before/after truncation)

The three layers of defense are now: top-K limits result count, threshold filters weak matches, truncation caps per-chunk size. All three are tested and documented.

What can you tell me about chunk overlap in the current configuration with the retrieval limit being a top 800 characters per chunk? even if the chunks are actually embedded or indexed, are they actually readable by the system? Or do we need to shrink the chunk embedding slash indexing parameters in order to capture the entire file footprint?
Good question. There's a real mismatch in the current setup. Let me get concrete data.

tools.name: Bash
Bash

turns.text:
Here's the picture:

**48% of chunks are over 800 chars.** 22% are over 2000. The worst case is 23.6KB — 97% of that content is embedded (influences which queries match it) but invisible to the meta-agent after truncation.

The problem is exactly what you're pointing at. There are two distinct issues:

**1. Phantom matching.** The embedding vector is computed from the full 4KB+ text. A query can match on content deep in the chunk — concepts, terms, patterns at char 2000 — and rank that chunk highly. But the meta-agent receives only the first 800 chars and never sees the content that caused the match. The retrieval looks good on paper (high similarity score) but the delivered context is a non-sequitur from the meta-agent's perspective.

**2. No overlap.** The chunker splits on `##` headings with zero overlap. Content that falls at a section boundary gets split across two chunks with no shared context. If a concept's setup is at the end of one section and its conclusion is at the start of the next, neither chunk is self-contained. This isn't as bad as the phantom matching problem — markdown headings are natural semantic boundaries — but it means information that crosses boundaries (like a principle stated in one section and applied in the next) exists in neither chunk as a complete thought.

**The fix lives in the chunker, not the retrieval layer.** Truncation at retrieval time was a necessary safety net (we can't send 7KB to a 4-8B model), but it's masking the real problem: the chunks are too big. The right approach:

- Cap chunks at ~800 chars during indexing
- When a `##` section exceeds that, split it further — at paragraph boundaries (`\n\n`), or at `###` sub-headings if they exist
- Add ~100 chars of overlap at split boundaries so the embedding captures context from both sides of the cut
- Re-index

That way the embedding vector accurately represents what the meta-agent will actually see, and the truncation in `queryIndex()` becomes a no-op safety net rather than a load-bearing filter that hides 90%+ of matched content.

Want me to update the chunker and re-index?

Let's plan out some different chunking strategies. You mentioned that the natural semantic boundary of markdown headings is natural. I just don't think it's occurring as often as we need it to delineate chunks of this size. So we need to think through some other strategies that might be options for this indexing and then do different rounds of indexing and compare the results of one index against another to see not just speculatively but practically what do the results look like and how well do they perform. So the first step is let's think through some other chunking and indexing strategies.

tools.name: Agent
tools.summary: Agent: Explore chunker and index code

Excellent research. Now let me design the plan with a Plan agent, synthesizing everything.
Good plan from the agent. Let me now read the critical files to verify alignment, then write the final plan.
Good. I now have everything I need. Let me write the final plan.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/rhhart/.claude/skills/handoff-test

# Handoff Test

## The Naive Reader

A fresh Claude instance with CLAUDE.md and tooling but nothing from this conversation. The only things that exist for it are what's written in persistent artifacts.

## Process

1. Identify what was created or modified this session. Read each artifact in full.
2. From the naive reader perspective, find implicit dependencies:
   - Session-local terminology used without definition
   - References to "the bug," "the approach," "what we discussed" without grounding
   - Decisions recorded without rationale (a future reader needs *why* to know when the decision expires)
   - Scope assumed but not stated
   - Next steps that depend on conversation memory
3. Report gaps. For each: what's missing, where it goes, concrete suggested text. Offer to apply fixes.

If the artifact passes, say so in one sentence.

Let me identify and read every artifact created or modified this session.

**Session artifacts:**
1. `tools/embedding/store.ts` — Vectra API bug fix
2. `tools/embedding/query.ts` — added chunk truncation
3. `tools/embedding/retrieval-quality-test.md` — new file (broader test results)
4. `tools/embedding/retrieval-tests.md` — added validation rules section
5. `design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md` — multiple edits
6. `.claude/plans/gleaming-percolating-graham.md` — chunking strategy plan
Now let me also read the PRD edits to check them.

Here's the handoff test report.

---

## Handoff Test Results

### 1. `tools/embedding/store.ts` — **Passes with one gap**

The fix on line 83 (`queryItems(vector, "", topK)`) has no inline comment explaining *why* the empty string is there. A naive reader sees an unexplained empty string parameter and might "clean it up."

**Gap:** No comment explaining the Vectra API signature mismatch.
**Where:** `store.ts:83`
**Suggested text:**
```ts
// Vectra signature: queryItems(vector, query, topK, filter?, isBm25?)
// Second param is BM25 text search (unused). Passing topK here
// silently returns unlimited results. See PRD "Vectra API contract".
const results = await this.index.queryItems(vector, "", topK);
```

---

### 2. `tools/embedding/query.ts` — **Passes**

Self-contained. The `MAX_CHUNK_CHARS` constant has a clear budget comment (lines 19-27) explaining *why* 800 chars, how the budget works, and what happens if it's exceeded. The truncation logic is straightforward. A naive reader can understand and modify this without session context.

---

### 3. `tools/embedding/retrieval-quality-test.md` — **Two gaps**

**Gap A:** The 10 queries are referenced by session topic (e.g., "Git-ship skill design") but the actual query strings aren't recorded. A future reader can't re-run the test without the exact queries.

**Where:** After the Results Summary table.
**Suggested fix:** Add a section listing the 10 query strings:
```markdown
## Query strings used

| # | Query |
|---|---|
| 1 | "Building a git commit/push/PR skill — learner designing CLI automation for developer workflow" |
| 2 | "Applying security deny rules to settings — learner's understanding of permission models and attack surfaces" |
...
```

**Gap B:** Line 6 says "685 chunks" but the actual index has 678. Minor discrepancy — the handoff prompt said 685, the index analysis found 678.

**Where:** Line 6
**Suggested fix:** Change `685` to `678`.

---

### 4. `tools/embedding/retrieval-tests.md` (validation rules section) — **Passes**

The new validation rules section is fully self-contained. Explains the three checks, *why* each matters, references the Vectra bug as concrete evidence, and includes runnable test commands. A naive reader gets everything they need.

One nit: line 29 says "~2000 characters" as the budget, but query.ts says ~2500 and 800/chunk × 3 = 2400. The numbers are inconsistent across artifacts (2000 here, 2500 in query.ts, 2500 in PRD). Not a blocker but could confuse.

---

### 5. `design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md` — **Passes with one gap**

The edits are well-grounded. The bug description (lines 13-17) explains what happened, why the tests still passed, and what was fixed. The new decisions table rows (lines 246-247) include full rationale. The architecture diagram correction (line 63) is accurate.

**Gap:** The PRD status (line 18-19) says "Next: update prompt.md with source attribution instruction, then wire retrieval into observer.sh pipeline" — but the actual next step is now the chunking strategy A/B test (the plan we just wrote). The status is stale relative to session end-state.

**Where:** Lines 18-20
**Suggested fix:** Add after the current "Next" line:
```
**Prerequisite discovered:** Chunking strategy must be right-sized
before wiring into observer — current chunks are too large (48% exceed
800 chars). See tools/embedding/retrieval-quality-test.md and the
chunking A/B test plan.
```

---

### 6. `.claude/plans/gleaming-percolating-graham.md` — **Passes**

Fully self-contained. Context section explains the problem, research basis, and why we're testing. Strategies are defined with expected behavior. Steps reference concrete files. Verification section is runnable. "What we're NOT doing" prevents scope creep. A naive reader can execute this plan without session context.

---

## Summary

| Artifact | Verdict | Gaps |
|---|---|---|
| store.ts | Pass with gap | Missing inline comment on empty-string param |
| query.ts | Pass | — |
| retrieval-quality-test.md | Two gaps | Query strings not recorded; chunk count mismatch |
| retrieval-tests.md | Pass | Minor budget number inconsistency (2000 vs 2500) |
| PRD | Pass with gap | "Next" status doesn't mention chunking prerequisite |
| Plan file | Pass | — |

Want me to apply the fixes?
yes
Now the PRD status update.

All five fixes applied:

1. **store.ts:83** — Added 3-line comment explaining the empty-string Vectra parameter
2. **retrieval-quality-test.md:6** — Fixed chunk count from 685 to 678
3. **retrieval-quality-test.md** — Added full query strings table (10 rows) so tests are reproducible
4. **retrieval-tests.md:29** — Fixed budget from ~2000 to ~2500 to match query.ts and PRD
5. **Plan file** — Added PRD update step with suggested status text noting the chunking prerequisite

# Handoff Prompt

Do not read files. Work from memory.

## Recipient

Fresh Claude Code instance. Has CLAUDE.md, memory files, full
tooling. Has nothing from this session.

## Assemble from memory

HANDOFF PROMPT — MetaClaude Embedding Index: Chunking Strategy A/B Test

Date: 2026-03-10
Previous session: ~2 hours, retrieval quality testing + bug fixes + chunking plan

=== GOAL ===

Build and run a chunking strategy A/B test for the MetaClaude embedding
index. The index powers a metacognitive observer agent that watches
Claude Code sessions and makes selective context injections. The
embedding index lets the observer retrieve relevant chunks from learner
state, design docs, notepad, and reference materials.

Current chunks are too large — 48% exceed 800 chars, 22% exceed 2000,
worst is 23.6KB. Chunks are embedded at full size but truncated to 800
chars at retrieval time, creating a phantom matching problem: the
embedding vector represents content the meta-agent never sees.

The fix: right-size chunks at indexing time, tested empirically across
multiple strategies.

=== WHAT'S DONE ===

1. BROADER RETRIEVAL QUALITY TEST — COMPLETE
   Extracted 10 real conversation windows from Claude Code session
   transcripts (March 3-7). Crafted Deep-mode queries for each. Ran
   against the index at top-3, threshold 0.6. Results: 8/10 actionable
   (3 excellent, 5 good, 2 mixed — content gaps not retrieval failures).
   Full results: tools/embedding/retrieval-quality-test.md

2. VECTRA topK BUG — FOUND AND FIXED
   Vectra's queryItems(vector, query, topK) signature has BM25 query
   string as second param. Our code passed queryItems(vector, topK) —
   topK was silently interpreted as a query string, returning unlimited
   results. Fixed in store.ts:83 (added empty string: queryItems(vector,
   "", topK)). Comment explains the fix. The original 7 handcrafted
   tests ran with this bug active — they validated content relevance
   (correct) but never caught the unbounded result count.

3. RETRIEVAL PAYLOAD TRUNCATION — IMPLEMENTED
   Added MAX_CHUNK_CHARS = 800 in query.ts. truncateChunk() cuts at
   last paragraph boundary before 800 chars, appends "[truncated — full
   content at source path]". Both text and formatted fields truncated.
   Payloads now range 1.3-2.1KB at top-3 (down from 1.3-7.4KB).

4. TEST METHODOLOGY UPGRADED
   retrieval-tests.md now has a "Validation rules" section requiring
   three checks: (1) result count matches top-K, (2) total payload
   stays within ~2500 char budget, (3) content relevance. Explains
   why all three matter — the Vectra bug demonstrated silent degradation.

5. PRD UPDATED
   design/2026-03-09-metaclaude-local-prd.md:
   - Status line documents the bug, fix, and corrected test methodology
   - Architecture diagram corrected from "SQLite-vec" to "Vectra"
   - Phase 2 build notes revised with bug context and threshold correction
   - Broader test marked complete with results summary
   - Decisions table: two new rows (retrieval payload budget, Vectra API contract)

6. CHUNKING PLAN — WRITTEN, NOT YET EXECUTED
   Plan file: .claude/plans/gleaming-percolating-graham.md
   Read this file first — it's the complete implementation spec.

=== WHAT'S NOT DONE (the plan) ===

Execute the plan in .claude/plans/gleaming-percolating-graham.md.
Summary of steps:

1. Add ChunkConfig type to chunker.ts (maxChars, overlapChars,
   contextualPrefix). Add config param to ChunkSource interface.

2. Implement chunkRecursiveMarkdown() in chunker.ts — split hierarchy:
   ## → ### → \n\n → sentence (". ") → hard-cut. Track heading stack
   for section metadata. Max 2000 chars, 200 char overlap.

3. Implement chunkFixedSize() in chunker.ts — sliding window at
   maxChars with overlap. Baseline comparator, no structure awareness.

4. Add contextual prefix post-processing in chunkSource() — prepend
   "From {source}, section '{section}':" to chunk.text when enabled.
   ~60 chars overhead per chunk.

5. Add CLI flags to index.ts: --index-path, --strategy, --max-chars,
   --overlap, --no-context. These let the benchmark drive indexing.

6. Build tools/embedding/benchmark.ts — the test harness:
   - 17 queries hardcoded (7 from retrieval-tests.md + 10 from
     retrieval-quality-test.md with query strings in the table)
   - 4 strategies: baseline (current), recursive-md (2000/200),
     recursive-md-ctx (2000/200 + prefix), fixed-800 (800/120)
   - For each: temp index, chunk stats, embed, run queries, record
     scores/hit rates/payload sizes
   - Output comparison table + per-query divergence detail
   - ~3-4 min runtime (4 × ~40s embedding)

7. Run benchmark, pick winner by: hit rate (primary), mean score
   (secondary), payload ≤2500 (must-pass), zero oversized chunks
   (must-pass).

8. Update production: set winning config as default, reindex with
   bun tools/embedding/index.ts --reindex, verify, update PRD status.

Embedding tools: tools/embedding/{store,embed,chunker,index,query}.ts

Broader test: tools/embedding/retrieval-quality-test.md (10 real-session + query strings)
